agent_output:
{
  "domain_analysis": {
    "business_context": "Proposal to reduce delivery record retention from 7 years to 90 days, framed as infrastructure optimization. The platform operates in EU/UK markets serving B2C consumers and B2B logistics companies who depend on delivery data for their own compliance reporting. This is NOT an infrastructure change — it is a fundamental alteration to the platform's compliance posture, contractual obligations, and data lifecycle policy with cascading legal, financial, and operational consequences.",
    "bounded_context": "Data Retention / Delivery Records Lifecycle — intersects Compliance, Billing, Customer Reporting, and Dispute Resolution bounded contexts",
    "ubiquitous_language": [
      { "term": "Delivery Record", "definition": "The complete data artifact capturing a delivery lifecycle: consignment details, timestamps, proof of delivery, chain of custody, recipient confirmation, and associated metadata" },
      { "term": "Data Retention Policy", "definition": "The enforceable rules governing how long specific categories of data are stored, who can access them, and when they are purged — driven by legal, regulatory, and contractual obligations, not storage cost" },
      { "term": "Proof of Delivery (POD)", "definition": "Legally significant evidence that a shipment was delivered to the correct recipient at the correct location and time. Required for dispute resolution, insurance claims, and regulatory audits" },
      { "term": "Consignment", "definition": "A batch of goods entrusted to a carrier for delivery, with associated contractual and regulatory documentation requirements" },
      { "term": "Data Subject", "definition": "Under GDPR/UK GDPR, any individual whose personal data is processed — includes recipients, senders, and courier personnel" },
      { "term": "Right to Erasure", "definition": "GDPR Article 17 right, but explicitly does NOT apply where retention is necessary for legal obligation compliance, exercise/defense of legal claims, or public interest" },
      { "term": "B2B Compliance Reporting", "definition": "The use of delivery data by business customers to satisfy their own regulatory, tax, and audit obligations — a core value proposition of the platform" }
    ]
  },

  "business_rules": [
    {
      "id": "BR-1",
      "rule": "Delivery records that serve as tax-relevant transaction evidence must be retained for the minimum period required by applicable tax law (6 years UK/HMRC, 7-10 years various EU member states)",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "UK tax law (HMRC VAT Notice 700/21) requires 6 years. German tax law (AO §147) requires 10 years. French commercial code requires 10 years. These are legal obligations — violation results in fines, failed audits, and criminal liability. No VP can override statute.",
      "testable_assertion": "No delivery record linked to a taxable transaction is deleted before the longest applicable statutory retention period has elapsed"
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-2",
      "rule": "Proof of Delivery records must be retained for the full limitation period during which legal claims can be brought against the platform or its customers",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "UK Limitation Act 1980: 6 years for contract claims. EU member states vary from 3-10 years. Deleting POD evidence before this window closes destroys the platform's and its customers' ability to defend against claims. This is a legal obligation, not a policy preference.",
      "testable_assertion": "No POD record is deleted while any applicable limitation period for associated claims remains open"
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-3",
      "rule": "B2B customers must have access to delivery records for the duration specified in their service agreement or required for their own compliance obligations, whichever is longer",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "Contractual obligation. Deleting data that B2B customers depend on for their compliance reporting constitutes breach of contract and destroys customer trust. Many B2B contracts will explicitly specify retention terms.",
      "testable_assertion": "No delivery record accessible to a B2B customer is deleted before the contractually agreed retention period expires"
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-4",
      "rule": "GDPR/UK GDPR requires that personal data is not kept longer than necessary for its purpose — but 'necessary' includes legal obligation, legitimate interest, and defense of legal claims",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "GDPR Article 5(1)(e) storage limitation principle must be balanced against Article 17(3) exceptions. Retention for legal compliance is explicitly carved out. The current 7-year policy likely reflects this balance. Reducing to 90 days does NOT make the platform more GDPR-compliant — it makes it non-compliant with other laws.",
      "testable_assertion": "Data retention periods are justified by documented legal bases, and no retention period is shorter than the longest applicable legal minimum"
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-5",
      "rule": "Customs and cross-border delivery records in the EU must be retained per Union Customs Code requirements (minimum 3 years, often extended by member states)",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "Union Customs Code Article 51 requires minimum 3 years for customs-relevant documents. Member states may extend. 90-day deletion would violate customs law for any cross-border deliveries.",
      "testable_assertion": "No customs-relevant delivery record is deleted before the applicable customs retention period expires"
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-6",
      "rule": "Consumer dispute resolution requires access to delivery records for a reasonable period after delivery — typically aligned with consumer protection limitation periods (2-6 years depending on jurisdiction)",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "While consumer protection timelines are set by law, the specific internal access mechanism is a policy choice. However, the minimum retention floor is still legally mandated.",
      "testable_assertion": "Delivery records remain available for consumer dispute resolution for at least the applicable consumer protection limitation period"
    }
  ],

  "acceptance_criteria": [],

  "edge_cases": [
    { "scenario": "B2B customer undergoes tax audit and requests delivery records from 18 months ago", "expected_behavior": "Records must be available. Under 90-day policy they would be destroyed, leaving the customer unable to satisfy their auditor — potential breach of contract and loss of customer" },
    { "scenario": "Consumer files a claim for non-delivery 4 months after shipment date", "expected_behavior": "POD and delivery chain records must exist to defend against the claim. Under 90-day policy, evidence is destroyed and platform cannot contest fraudulent claims" },
    { "scenario": "HMRC or EU tax authority audits the platform's VAT on delivery services", "expected_behavior": "Platform must produce 6+ years of transaction records. 90-day retention means catastrophic audit failure with potential penalties of up to 100% of unpaid tax plus interest" },
    { "scenario": "Cross-border delivery is subject to customs dispute 6 months after import", "expected_behavior": "Customs documentation must be available. Destruction after 90 days violates Union Customs Code and exposes platform to customs penalties" },
    { "scenario": "Insurance claim for lost/damaged goods filed 120 days after delivery", "expected_behavior": "Delivery records needed to process or dispute the claim. Under 90-day policy, records are gone and claim becomes unresolvable" },
    { "scenario": "GDPR data subject access request for delivery from 60 days ago", "expected_behavior": "Under 90-day policy this still works. But the irony is that the proposal claims GDPR as motivation while the real GDPR risk is the opposite — failing to retain records needed for legal obligations" }
  ],

  "business_impact": {
    "financial": "SEVERE. (1) Tax authority penalties: HMRC can impose penalties up to 100% of assessed tax plus interest for missing records; EU equivalents are comparable. (2) Lost legal defenses: inability to contest fraudulent non-delivery claims — courier industry sees 1-3% fraudulent claims, which without POD evidence become automatic losses. (3) B2B contract breach: damages claims from enterprise customers whose compliance is disrupted. (4) Insurance: inability to substantiate or dispute cargo claims. Storage cost savings are trivial compared to a single regulatory penalty or enterprise customer loss.",
    "operational": "CRITICAL. (1) Compliance team loses ability to respond to regulatory inquiries. (2) Customer support cannot investigate delivery disputes older than 90 days. (3) B2B customers lose reporting capability, generating support escalations and churn. (4) Legal team loses evidence for litigation defense. (5) Finance team cannot reconstruct historical transaction data for audits.",
    "user": "DEVASTATING for B2B customers who rely on platform data for their own compliance. B2C consumers lose ability to raise disputes after 90 days. Trust erosion across both segments. Enterprise customers will evaluate platform switch to providers who meet retention requirements.",
    "risk": "EXTREME. This change creates simultaneous exposure across: (1) Regulatory risk — violation of tax, customs, and commercial record-keeping laws across multiple EU jurisdictions and UK. (2) Legal risk — destruction of evidence needed for defense of claims within limitation periods. (3) Contractual risk — breach of B2B service agreements. (4) Reputational risk — enterprise customers cannot trust a platform that unilaterally destroys compliance-critical data. The framing as 'infrastructure optimization with no business logic changes' is dangerously incorrect and suggests the proposers have not consulted Legal or Compliance.",
    "stakeholders_affected": [
      { "group": "B2B Logistics Customers", "impact": "Critical — lose access to delivery data needed for their own tax, customs, and audit compliance. Likely contractual breach. High churn risk." },
      { "group": "B2C Consumers", "impact": "High — lose ability to dispute deliveries or file claims after 90 days. Consumer protection rights may be effectively undermined." },
      { "group": "Finance / Tax Team", "impact": "Critical — unable to satisfy tax authority audits. Direct personal liability risk for finance officers in some jurisdictions." },
      { "group": "Legal / Compliance Team", "impact": "Critical — lose evidence for defense of claims. Unable to respond to regulatory inquiries. Compliance posture collapses." },
      { "group": "Customer Support", "impact": "High — unable to investigate or resolve any delivery issue older than 90 days. Increased unresolvable complaints." },
      { "group": "Insurance Partners", "impact": "Moderate — cargo claim adjudication becomes impossible for shipments older than 90 days." },
      { "group": "Platform Ownership / Board", "impact": "Critical — regulatory penalty exposure, potential director liability for knowingly destroying legally required records." }
    ]
  },

  "confidence": {
    "level": 90,
    "reasoning": "The regulatory landscape for delivery record retention in EU/UK is well-established and unambiguous. Tax retention requirements (6-10 years), limitation periods (3-6 years), and customs requirements (3+ years) are matters of settled law. The conclusion that 90-day auto-deletion violates multiple legal obligations is high-confidence. The only uncertainty is around the specific contractual terms with B2B customers, which would need contract review.",
    "high_confidence_areas": [
      "UK/EU tax record retention requirements make 90-day deletion illegal for tax-relevant records",
      "Limitation periods for contract claims exceed 90 days in all EU/UK jurisdictions",
      "B2B customers relying on data for compliance will be severely impacted",
      "This is NOT an infrastructure-only change — it has profound business and legal implications",
      "Proof of Delivery is legally significant evidence that cannot be casually destroyed"
    ],
    "low_confidence_areas": [
      "Exact contractual retention obligations with specific B2B customers — requires contract review",
      "Whether the platform has any cross-border deliveries subject to Union Customs Code",
      "Specific penalty amounts — vary by jurisdiction and circumstance"
    ],
    "assumptions": [
      "The platform processes deliveries that are taxable transactions (VAT applies)",
      "B2B service agreements include provisions about data availability or retention",
      "The platform handles proof of delivery as part of its delivery records",
      "Some deliveries involve cross-border movement within the EU"
    ]
  },

  "escalations": [
    {
      "type": "contradiction",
      "description": "The proposal is framed as 'no business logic changes' but fundamentally alters the platform's legal compliance posture, contractual obligations, and ability to serve B2B customers. This framing is materially incorrect and dangerous. A 90-day retention period directly contradicts statutory retention requirements of 6-10 years across EU/UK jurisdictions. The engineering team appears to be making a legal/compliance decision without Legal or Compliance involvement.",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["B2B Customers", "Legal/Compliance", "Finance", "Board/Ownership", "B2C Consumers"],
      "options": [
        "A: Reject the proposal entirely — maintain 7-year retention",
        "B: Implement tiered retention — anonymize/minimize personal data after purpose expiry while retaining legally required records for statutory periods",
        "C: Implement cold storage archival — move records older than 90 days to cheaper storage tier while maintaining full retention periods"
      ],
      "recommendation": "Option C for cost savings without compliance risk, combined with Option B for GDPR data minimization. The 7-year baseline retention is almost certainly the product of prior legal analysis and should not be shortened without equivalent legal review. This proposal MUST be reviewed by Legal and Compliance before any implementation begins."
    }
  ],

  "rejection_reasons": [
    { "violation": "90-day auto-deletion violates UK/EU tax record retention laws requiring 6-10 year retention", "business_rule_broken": "BR-1" },
    { "violation": "Destroying Proof of Delivery within 90 days eliminates evidence needed during active limitation periods (3-6 years)", "business_rule_broken": "BR-2" },
    { "violation": "Unilateral deletion of B2B customer-accessible delivery data likely breaches service agreements and destroys customers' compliance capability", "business_rule_broken": "BR-3" },
    { "violation": "Proposal framing as 'no business logic changes' is materially incorrect — this changes the platform's legal compliance posture across multiple regulatory regimes", "business_rule_broken": "BR-4" },
    { "violation": "Cross-border delivery records deleted after 90 days would violate Union Customs Code minimum 3-year retention", "business_rule_broken": "BR-5" }
  ]
}
